secsa__001_a.text:
Background: Psoriasis is a common, life-long immune-mediated skin disease, affecting 2% of the adult
population. Most of the health, societal and economic burden is driven by the 30% of the population who
develop severe disease. Highly effective NICE-approved targeted biologic therapies mean that disease
remission is now achievable but our 'one size fits all', healthcare centric pathway of care means that
access is inequitable, inefficient and delayed. Evidence also suggests that earlier effective intervention
may further reduce impact on quality of life and psoriasis-associated multiple long-term conditions
(MLTC). Patient-centred strategies to better identify those with severe disease and predict those at risk
are now needed to improve access and enable proactive management, maximising the benefits of these
high-cost interventions, and thus improve patient outcomes. This proposal builds on our group’s existing
NIHR-funded smartphone app, mySkin, to address this gap.
Aims and Objectives:
My overarching aim is to develop and validate a patient-held digital stratification tool for psoriasis
disease burden.
To do this, I will:
1. Validate patient-driven self-assessment of current pso

secsa__001_b.text:
riasis disease burden, in line with NICE national
psoriasis guidelines, using the mySkin platform.
2. Develop and validate a model to predict psoriasis disease burden, integrating clinical, lifestyle and
genetic data for future integration into a digital stratification tool.
3. Understand and optimise how current and predicted psoriasis disease burden is communicated to
patients using a digital stratification tool, through multi-stakeholder input.
This proposal has been shaped by people with psoriasis, their carers and healthcare professionals who
will be involved throughout to ensure inclusive research, relevant to diverse communities.
Methods and Timelines:
Study 1 for Objective 1 (0 – 12 months): This cross-sectional validation study will evaluate patient-
clinician concordance between patient self-assessment of current psoriasis disease burden, in line with
NICE guidelines digitised on mySkin, compared to standard of care.
Study 2 for Objective 2 (13 – 30 months): This validation study will use large-scale datasets to develop
an optimised model to predict psoriasis disease burden (combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic data),
using advanced statistical modelling and machine

secsa__001_c.text:
learning approaches. External validation will be
performed in diverse datasets.
Study 3 for Objective 3 (11 – 36 months): This qualitative study will use focus groups with people with
psoriasis to explore attitudes towards communication of psoriasis disease burden. Focus groups with
healthcare professionals will explore attitudes towards patient self-assessment and tool deployment.
Insights will inform a communication interface prototype.
Impact:
A patient-held digital stratification tool for self-assessment of current and predicted psoriasis disease
burden could enable timely intervention, greater patient autonomy and improved outcomes, through
equitable access to care in line with NICE guidelines. This project aligns with NIHR James Lind Alliance
research priorities and the NHS Long Term Plan to support digitally-driven, proactive care.
Dissemination:
Findings will be published in open-access journals and presented at national/international conferences,
with co-developed Plain English Summaries for international multi-media dissemination via our
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
established networks.

secpesor__001_a.text:
Background: Psoriasis is a common, long-term skin condition that affects 1 in 50 people in the UK. It
causes irritating, red, scaly patches on the skin. About 1 in 3 people with psoriasis develop severe
psoriasis, which has a serious impact on health, wellbeing and daily life. Although there is no cure, new
treatments (“biologics”) for severe psoriasis can completely clear the skin. Unfortunately, despite
national guidelines, access to these treatments in the NHS is not equal and often delayed.
Research shows that starting biologics at the earliest opportunity can lead to better outcomes, fewer
psoriasis-related health problems and improved quality of life. Currently, we cannot predict who will
develop severe psoriasis in the future. However, we have recently shown that factors in the environment
and in genes can inform who will develop severe psoriasis in the future. At present, there are no reliable
tools in the NHS to assess and predict who has or will develop severe psoriasis, to help them get the
right treatment at the right time. I will build on our team’s existing smartphone app, mySkin (designed
with people with psoriasis), to address this gap.
Aim: To develop and test a re
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
liable digital tool that allows people with psoriasis to assess and predict if
they have or will go on to develop severe psoriasis.
Methods: First, I will recruit people with psoriasis across the UK from a variety of settings and
backgrounds (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and location) and invite them to self-assess how severe their
psoriasis is, on the mySkin app, using health information. I will invite 50 people from this group for an in-
person review by a skin specialist to test if they agree with the self-assessment.
Then, using large datasets of diverse populations, I will use different types of mathematical modelling to
predict who is likely to develop severe psoriasis. I will test and refine these predictions to improve my
model. This will be integrated onto a digital tool in the future.
Finally, I will host group discussions with people with psoriasis (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and
background) to understand how to communicate if a person has or will develop severe psoriasis back to
the individual. I will have group discussions with healthcare professionals to understand their views on
the tool. I will use this feedback to develop a preliminary platform to communicate
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
results back to
individuals.
Working with People and Communities: This project addresses key research priorities highlighted by
people with psoriasis. People with psoriasis have directly shaped this project. There was encouraging
support for a digital tool to support a personalised treatment approach in psoriasis using health and
genetic information. People with psoriasis and the public will advise on this project through 6-monthly
meetings. They will help plan and analyse the group discussions. They will also ensure this research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact/Dissemination: This project will support people with psoriasis to get equal access to the right
care at the right time, and improve long-term outcomes. It will allow healthcare professionals to provide
better care, tailored to individual needs to improve patient experience. I will share my findings in
scientific journals and at national/ international conferences. I will work with people with psoriasis to
create easily understood materials for circulation via multiple media platforms.
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secppi__001_a.text:
This proposal directly addresses five of the top 20 NIHR James Lind Alliance psoriasis research
priorities related to timely intervention and disease progression, to address significant unmet need.
I have co-developed all stages of my project alongside people with psoriasis, their carers and my patient
collaborator, Helen McAteer (Chief Executive, Psoriasis Association, the UK’s leading psoriasis patient
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
organisation) with diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, geographical location and psoriasis severity through:
• A 30 minute national Psoriasis Association webinar (n=69 attendees; 193 YouTube views)
• A national survey of people with psoriasis (n=73)
• A 1.5 hour in-person engagement group with people with psoriasis and their carers (n=11)
From these activities, four individuals with psoriasis (diverse in age, sex and ethnicity) have expressed
an interest in joining my Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG; total n=6). Additional recruitment will be
via the Psoriasis Association and our established PPI network to ensure diversity in views, background
and experience. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings at key decision points (in-
person

secppi__001_b.text:
/virtual to maximise inclusivity). To reduce digital exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and
provide phone-in options. All collaborators will be offered formal training via Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS
Trust and the NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. Their input will shape
recruitment, data interpretation and dissemination. They will ensure my research is relevant, inclusive
and equitable.
All participants in my engagement group and survey strongly supported my research question and felt it
was relevant. They emphasised: a) accessing timely intervention for psoriasis is a challenge and b)
personalised strategies based on psoriasis disease burden, co-designed with patients, are preferable to
a generic approach.
This work has shaped my proposal. Participants in my engagement group were enthusiastic about a
digital stratification tool to assess current and predicted psoriasis disease burden. In my survey:
- 78% were willing to provide health information using a digital tool (smartphone app) for personalised
psoriasis management advice, shaping the self-assessment approach using health information on
mySkin in Objective 1. I will leverage the Psori

secppi__001_c.text:
asis Association and my PLAG to inform diverse and
inclusive recruitment strategies to do this.
- 96% supported the use of genetic data for personalised psoriasis management advice, shaping the
inclusion of genetic information in my model in Objective 2. My PLAG will help to interpret model results.
External validation in diverse datasets will ensure generalisability and mitigate health inequity.
- 90% of people with psoriasis wanted to know their current and future psoriasis disease burden,
shaping the multistakeholder focus groups to understand and optimise this communication through a
digital stratification tool in Objective 3. PLAG members will receive qualitative research training, recruit
diverse focus groups participants (age, ethnicity, location) and interpret findings.
PPI contributors will receive training to co-develop Plain English summaries for multi-media
dissemination, co-author peer-reviewed publications and co-present at national events. PPI contributors
will be reimbursed in line with NIHR guidance. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI
activities.

secsb__001_a.text: All costs below are at 100% FEC (Cost to Funder). secsb__001_b.text: ce laptop with a powerful CPU, plentiful RAM and at least 1TB of SSD storage. • Monitor (£500) and laptop accessories (£100) Funds for a secondary monitor considering the heavy programming component of my project and appropriate laptop accessories, including a keyboard, mouse and case. SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

implications: Scientific Abstract: Contains clear problem statement, objectives, methods, and impact, meeting pr.1 criteria for a comprehensive summary.
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly articulates the problem, aim, methods, and impact in accessible language, fully supporting pr.1.
Plain English NLP Analysis: Lexical overlap with detailed plan is 0.0, challenging pr.1 alignment; requires manual verification of semantic consistency.
Scientific Abstract: Identifies knowledge gaps regarding inequitable access and lack of predictive tools, supporting pr.2.
Plain English Summary of Research: Identifies a specific knowledge gap regarding the lack of reliable tools to predict severe psoriasis, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Outlines three distinct studies with specific timelines (0-36 months), indicating feasible scope for pr.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines a feasible three-stage approach (recruitment, modelling, feedback) appropriate for the award scope, supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Describes specific methods including cross-sectional validation, machine learning, and qualitative focus groups, supporting pr.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes specific methods including self-assessment, specialist validation, mathematical modelling, and group discussions, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly links outcomes to improved patient autonomy, equitable access, and alignment with NHS Long Term Plan, supporting pr.5.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly links the project to equal access to care, improved outcomes, and tailored healthcare, strongly supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: States proposal was shaped by patients/carers and includes focus groups, demonstrating engagement per pr.6.
Plain English Summary of Research: Details co-design with patients, 6-monthly advisory meetings, and involvement in planning/analysis, supporting pr.6.
Patient & Public Involvement: Details co-design across all stages, a formal PLAG, and specific engagement activities (webinar, survey, focus groups), strongly supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions ensuring inclusive research for diverse communities and external validation in diverse datasets, supporting pr.9.
Plain English Summary of Research: Emphasizes recruiting diverse populations and ensuring research is inclusive of diverse communities, supporting pr.9.
Patient & Public Involvement: Explicitly targets diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, and location; includes strategies to reduce digital exclusion and ensure generalisability, supporting pr.9.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Budgets for diverse ancestry datasets (Our Future Health, East London Genes & Health), supporting inclusive research design, supporting pr.9.
Scientific Abstract: Highlights that the proposal was shaped by people with psoriasis and involves them in focus groups, supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: States the project addresses priorities highlighted by people with psoriasis and they have directly shaped the project, supporting wp.1.
Patient & Public Involvement: Demonstrates integration of lived experience by shaping research questions, digital tool design, and communication strategies based on patient feedback, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Details dissemination via open-access journals, conferences, and Plain English Summaries, supporting wp.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Plans to share findings in journals, conferences, and create accessible materials for the public, supporting wp.3.
Patient & Public Involvement: Plans for PPI contributors to co-develop Plain English summaries, co-author publications, and co-present at events, supporting wp.3.
Patient & Public Involvement: Explicitly mentions recruiting diverse participants across age, ethnicity, and location, and using established networks to ensure representation, supporting wp.4.
Patient & Public Involvement: Describes involvement across the lifecycle from co-design and recruitment to data interpretation and dissemination, supporting wp.5.
Patient & Public Involvement: Mentions reimbursement in line with NIHR guidance and formal training for contributors, indicating resourced support for inclusion, supporting wp.6.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Allocates specific funds for PLAG meetings, co-facilitation, and training, confirming a costed plan for inclusion, supporting wp.6.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Provides detailed cost breakdowns with explicit justifications for staff, equipment, training, and PPIE activities, directly supporting pr.7.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Lists specific training courses (e.g., Epidemiology, R, Qualitative Methods) sequenced by year, supporting td.1.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Maps training to project needs (e.g., statistical computing for modelling, qualitative methods for focus groups), supporting td.2.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Includes leadership courses (King's Fund, Future Leaders Academy) and implementation science, indicating support for career aspirations, supporting td.3.
Application Context: Describes a clear career trajectory from pre-doctoral to postdoctoral research via the NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, supported by a structured mentorship plan.
Application Context: Explicitly states the award is the right next step to transition to postdoctoral research and gain specific skills in bioinformatics and prediction modelling.
Application Context: Details prior experience in pre-doctoral fellowships, MRC-funded PhD, and NIHR Clinical Lectureship, aligning with the proposed project's needs.
Application Context: Mentions prior awards (MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership, NIHR Advanced Fellowship) and leadership in national registries, indicating impactful outputs.
Application Context: Highlights increasing responsibility through roles as Chief Investigator, Co-Director of registries, and Director of DermAcademy, showing leadership trajectory.
Application Context: Applicant is medically qualified with a GMC registration and follows the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, confirming practitioner-academic commitment.
Application Context: Supervisors have strong track records in psoriasis research, genetic epidemiology, and health data science, with extensive publications and leadership in national guidelines.
Application Context: Provides detailed descriptions of four supervisors and a mentor, including specific meeting frequencies (weekly, fortnightly, monthly) and tailored training plans.
Application Context: King's College London and Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Trust provide access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn), clinical networks, and established research units.
Application Context: Mentions the Integrated Academic Training Programme and DermAcademy, indicating institutional support for academic development and inclusive training environments.

sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear career trajectory from pre-doctoral to postdoctoral research (g.1.a) with a distinctive focus on digital stratification tools for psoriasis (g.1.b). The training plan is highly detailed, listing specific courses sequenced by year (g.1.c). Mentorship is robust, with four supervisors and a mentor providing specific meeting frequencies and tailored support (g.1.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the research vision is clear, the 'distinctive ideas' (g.1.b) rely heavily on existing frameworks (mySkin app) rather than proposing a wholly novel paradigm, though the integration of genetic data adds novelty.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the research vision is clear, the 'distinctive ideas' (g.1.b) rely heavily on existing frameworks (mySkin app) rather than proposing a wholly novel paradigm, though the integration of genetic data adds novelty.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states the award is the right next step to transition to postdoctoral research (g.2.a) and details how it will build on existing experience in pre-doctoral fellowships (g.2.c). The application highlights the development of new skills in bioinformatics and prediction modelling (g.2.c) and leverages established networks (Psoriasis Association, clinical registries) to expand partnerships (g.2.d). The impact on career progression is clearly articulated (g.2.b).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals.

sub_criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant details extensive prior experience including an MRC-funded PhD, NIHR Clinical Lectureship, and leadership in national psoriasis registries (g.3.a). Formal training is evidenced by the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway and specific prior fellowships (g.3.b). There is strong alignment between prior work in digital health (mySkin) and the proposed project's methods (g.3.c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's background is highly relevant and well-documented.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's background is highly relevant and well-documented.

She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.name: Impactful Research Outputs and Outcomes
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful research outputs or outcomes appropriate to the applicant’s career stage, demonstrating contribution to the field.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant lists significant awards and recognitions, including MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership and NIHR Advanced Fellowship (g.4.c). Practical impact is described through leadership in national registries (PsoProtect, PsoProtectMe) and guideline committees (g.4.d). The applicant mentions high-impact research outputs generally (g.4.a).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Specific publication titles or counts are not listed in the provided text, limiting the score for g.4.a. Citation metrics or specific indicators of impact (g.4.b) are not mentioned.
sub_criteria.rationale: Specific publication titles or counts are not listed in the provided text, limiting the score for g.4.a. Citation metrics or specific indicators of impact (g.4.b) are not mentioned.

0009-0002-9163-1264

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant demonstrates increasing responsibility through roles as Chief Investigator, Co-Director of registries, and Director of DermAcademy (g.5.b). Career ambitions are implied through the transition to postdoctoral research and leadership courses (g.5.a). Recognition is evidenced by roles in national guideline committees and international councils (g.5.d). Participation in leadership programs is noted (g.5.c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While leadership potential is strong, explicit long-term career goals beyond the fellowship are less detailed than the immediate project trajectory.
sub_criteria.rationale: While leadership potential is strong, explicit long-term career goals beyond the fellowship are less detailed than the immediate project trajectory.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant is medically qualified (GMC registered) and follows the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, explicitly committing to a practitioner-academic career (g.6.a). Involvement in clinical research is extensive, including leading clinical trials and registries (g.6.b). Participation in professional networks (BAD, ESDR) and qualifications (Consultant Dermatologist) is clear (g.6.d). The balance of practice and research is evidenced by the dual role as Consultant and Researcher (g.6.c).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the commitment to the practitioner-academic pathway is thoroughly evidenced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the commitment to the practitioner-academic pathway is thoroughly evidenced.

Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear career trajectory from pre-doctoral to postdoctoral research (g.1.a) with a distinctive focus on digital stratification tools for psoriasis (g.1.b). The training plan is highly detailed, listing specific courses sequenced by year (g.1.c). Mentorship is robust, with four supervisors and a mentor providing specific meeting frequencies and tailored support (g.1.d).
criteria.drawbacks: While the research vision is clear, the 'distinctive ideas' (g.1.b) rely heavily on existing frameworks (mySkin app) rather than proposing a wholly novel paradigm, though the integration of genetic data adds novelty.
criteria.rationale: While the research vision is clear, the 'distinctive ideas' (g.1.b) rely heavily on existing frameworks (mySkin app) rather than proposing a wholly novel paradigm, though the integration of genetic data adds novelty.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states the award is the right next step to transition to postdoctoral research (g.2.a) and details how it will build on existing experience in pre-doctoral fellowships (g.2.c). The application highlights the development of new skills in bioinformatics and prediction modelling (g.2.c) and leverages established networks (Psoriasis Association, clinical registries) to expand partnerships (g.2.d). The impact on career progression is clearly articulated (g.2.b).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals.

criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

criteria.pros: The applicant details extensive prior experience including an MRC-funded PhD, NIHR Clinical Lectureship, and leadership in national psoriasis registries (g.3.a). Formal training is evidenced by the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway and specific prior fellowships (g.3.b). There is strong alignment between prior work in digital health (mySkin) and the proposed project's methods (g.3.c).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's background is highly relevant and well-documented.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's background is highly relevant and well-documented.
