His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

criteria.pros: The applicant lists significant awards and recognitions, including MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership and NIHR Advanced Fellowship (g.4.c). Practical impact is described through leadership in national registries (PsoProtect, PsoProtectMe) and guideline committees (g.4.d). The applicant mentions high-impact research outputs generally (g.4.a).

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

criteria.pros: The applicant demonstrates increasing responsibility through roles as Chief Investigator, Co-Director of registries, and Director of DermAcademy (g.5.b). Career ambitions are implied through the transition to postdoctoral research and leadership courses (g.5.a). Recognition is evidenced by roles in national guideline committees and international councils (g.5.d). Participation in leadership programs is noted (g.5.c).
criteria.drawbacks: While leadership potential is strong, explicit long-term career goals beyond the fellowship are less detailed than the immediate project trajectory.
criteria.rationale: While leadership potential is strong, explicit long-term career goals beyond the fellowship are less detailed than the immediate project trajectory.

criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

criteria.pros: The applicant is medically qualified (GMC registered) and follows the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, explicitly committing to a practitioner-academic career (g.6.a). Involvement in clinical research is extensive, including leading clinical trials and registries (g.6.b). Participation in professional networks (BAD, ESDR) and qualifications (Consultant Dermatologist) is clear (g.6.d). The balance of practice and research is evidenced by the dual role as Consultant and Researcher (g.6.c).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the commitment to the practitioner-academic pathway is thoroughly evidenced.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the commitment to the practitioner-academic pathway is thoroughly evidenced.

We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

evidence.text:
Background: Psoriasis is a common, long-term skin condition that affects 1 in 50 people in the UK. It
causes irritating, red, scaly patches on the skin. About 1 in 3 people with psoriasis develop severe
psoriasis, which has a serious impact on health, wellbeing and daily life. Although there is no cure, new
treatments (“biologics”) for severe psoriasis can completely clear the skin. Unfortunately, despite
national guidelines, access to these treatments in the NHS is not equal and often delayed.
Research shows that starting biologics at the earliest opportunity can lead to better outcomes, fewer
psoriasis-related health problems and improved quality of life. Currently, we cannot predict who will
develop severe psoriasis in the future. However, we have recently shown that factors in the environment
and in genes can inform who will develop severe psoriasis in the future. At present, there are no reliable
tools in the NHS to assess and predict who has or will develop severe psoriasis, to help them get the
right treatment at the right time. I will build on our team’s existing smartphone app, mySkin (designed
with people with psoriasis), to address this gap.
Aim: To develop and test a re

liable digital tool that allows people with psoriasis to assess and predict if
they have or will go on to develop severe psoriasis.
Methods: First, I will recruit people with psoriasis across the UK from a variety of settings and
backgrounds (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and location) and invite them to self-assess how severe their
psoriasis is, on the mySkin app, using health information. I will invite 50 people from this group for an in-
person review by a skin specialist to test if they agree with the self-assessment.
Then, using large datasets of diverse populations, I will use different types of mathematical modelling to
predict who is likely to develop severe psoriasis. I will test and refine these predictions to improve my
model. This will be integrated onto a digital tool in the future.
Finally, I will host group discussions with people with psoriasis (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and
background) to understand how to communicate if a person has or will develop severe psoriasis back to
the individual. I will have group discussions with healthcare professionals to understand their views on
the tool. I will use this feedback to develop a preliminary platform to communicate

Working with People and Communities: This project addresses key research priorities highlighted by
people with psoriasis. People with psoriasis have directly shaped this project. There was encouraging
support for a digital tool to support a personalised treatment approach in psoriasis using health and
genetic information. People with psoriasis and the public will advise on this project through 6-monthly
meetings. They will help plan and analyse the group discussions. They will also ensure this research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact/Dissemination: This project will support people with psoriasis to get equal access to the right
care at the right time, and improve long-term outcomes. It will allow healthcare professionals to provide
better care, tailored to individual needs to improve patient experience. I will share my findings in
scientific journals and at national/ international conferences. I will work with people with psoriasis to
create easily understood materials for circulation via multiple media platforms.

evidence.text:
Background: Psoriasis is a common, life-long immune-mediated skin disease, affecting 2% of the adult
population. Most of the health, societal and economic burden is driven by the 30% of the population who
develop severe disease. Highly effective NICE-approved targeted biologic therapies mean that disease
remission is now achievable but our 'one size fits all', healthcare centric pathway of care means that
access is inequitable, inefficient and delayed. Evidence also suggests that earlier effective intervention
may further reduce impact on quality of life and psoriasis-associated multiple long-term conditions
(MLTC). Patient-centred strategies to better identify those with severe disease and predict those at risk
are now needed to improve access and enable proactive management, maximising the benefits of these
high-cost interventions, and thus improve patient outcomes. This proposal builds on our group’s existing
NIHR-funded smartphone app, mySkin, to address this gap.
Aims and Objectives:
My overarching aim is to develop and validate a patient-held digital stratification tool for psoriasis
disease burden.
To do this, I will:
1. Validate patient-driven self-assessment of current pso

sub_criteria.definition: Whether the project scope matches the scale and expectations of the proposed award (doctoral, postdoctoral, etc.).

signals.signal_text: Alignment of aims with award level
Feasibility indicators (time, complexity, tasks)
Proportion of work relative to award expectations

sub_criteria.pros: The scope is perfectly aligned with a 3-year Doctoral Fellowship. The proposal outlines three distinct, manageable studies with clear timelines (0-12, 13-30, 11-36 months). The tasks (validation, modelling, qualitative feedback) are appropriate for the award level and leverage existing infrastructure (mySkin app, established datasets).

evidence.text:
riasis disease burden, in line with NICE national
psoriasis guidelines, using the mySkin platform.
2. Develop and validate a model to predict psoriasis disease burden, integrating clinical, lifestyle and
genetic data for future integration into a digital stratification tool.
3. Understand and optimise how current and predicted psoriasis disease burden is communicated to
patients using a digital stratification tool, through multi-stakeholder input.
This proposal has been shaped by people with psoriasis, their carers and healthcare professionals who
will be involved throughout to ensure inclusive research, relevant to diverse communities.
Methods and Timelines:
Study 1 for Objective 1 (0 – 12 months): This cross-sectional validation study will evaluate patient-
clinician concordance between patient self-assessment of current psoriasis disease burden, in line with
NICE guidelines digitised on mySkin, compared to standard of care.
Study 2 for Objective 2 (13 – 30 months): This validation study will use large-scale datasets to develop
an optimised model to predict psoriasis disease burden (combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic data),
using advanced statistical modelling and machine

learning approaches. External validation will be
performed in diverse datasets.
Study 3 for Objective 3 (11 – 36 months): This qualitative study will use focus groups with people with
psoriasis to explore attitudes towards communication of psoriasis disease burden. Focus groups with
healthcare professionals will explore attitudes towards patient self-assessment and tool deployment.
Insights will inform a communication interface prototype.
Impact:
A patient-held digital stratification tool for self-assessment of current and predicted psoriasis disease
burden could enable timely intervention, greater patient autonomy and improved outcomes, through
equitable access to care in line with NICE guidelines. This project aligns with NIHR James Lind Alliance
research priorities and the NHS Long Term Plan to support digitally-driven, proactive care.
Dissemination:
Findings will be published in open-access journals and presented at national/international conferences,
with co-developed Plain English Summaries for international multi-media dissemination via our
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
established networks.

evidence.text:
This proposal directly addresses five of the top 20 NIHR James Lind Alliance psoriasis research
priorities related to timely intervention and disease progression, to address significant unmet need.
I have co-developed all stages of my project alongside people with psoriasis, their carers and my patient
collaborator, Helen McAteer (Chief Executive, Psoriasis Association, the UK’s leading psoriasis patient
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
organisation) with diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, geographical location and psoriasis severity through:
• A 30 minute national Psoriasis Association webinar (n=69 attendees; 193 YouTube views)
• A national survey of people with psoriasis (n=73)
• A 1.5 hour in-person engagement group with people with psoriasis and their carers (n=11)
From these activities, four individuals with psoriasis (diverse in age, sex and ethnicity) have expressed
an interest in joining my Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG; total n=6). Additional recruitment will be
via the Psoriasis Association and our established PPI network to ensure diversity in views, background
and experience. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings at key decision points (in-
person

asis Association and my PLAG to inform diverse and
inclusive recruitment strategies to do this.
- 96% supported the use of genetic data for personalised psoriasis management advice, shaping the
inclusion of genetic information in my model in Objective 2. My PLAG will help to interpret model results.
External validation in diverse datasets will ensure generalisability and mitigate health inequity.
- 90% of people with psoriasis wanted to know their current and future psoriasis disease burden,
shaping the multistakeholder focus groups to understand and optimise this communication through a
digital stratification tool in Objective 3. PLAG members will receive qualitative research training, recruit
diverse focus groups participants (age, ethnicity, location) and interpret findings.
PPI contributors will receive training to co-develop Plain English summaries for multi-media
dissemination, co-author peer-reviewed publications and co-present at national events. PPI contributors
will be reimbursed in line with NIHR guidance. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI
activities.

sub_criteria.name: Justification of Resources & Award Costs

evidence.text:
Background: Psoriasis is a common, life-long immune-mediated skin disease, affecting 2% of the adult
population. Most of the health, societal and economic burden is driven by the 30% of the population who
develop severe disease. Highly effective NICE-approved targeted biologic therapies mean that disease
remission is now achievable but our 'one size fits all', healthcare centric pathway of care means that
access is inequitable, inefficient and delayed. Evidence also suggests that earlier effective intervention
may further reduce impact on quality of life and psoriasis-associated multiple long-term conditions
(MLTC). Patient-centred strategies to better identify those with severe disease and predict those at risk
are now needed to improve access and enable proactive management, maximising the benefits of these
high-cost interventions, and thus improve patient outcomes. This proposal builds on our group’s existing
NIHR-funded smartphone app, mySkin, to address this gap.
Aims and Objectives:
My overarching aim is to develop and validate a patient-held digital stratification tool for psoriasis
disease burden.
To do this, I will:
1. Validate patient-driven self-assessment of current pso

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

3. Understand and optimise how current and predicted psoriasis disease burden is communicated to

Study 1 for Objective 1 (0 – 12 months): This cross-sectional validation study will evaluate patient-
clinician concordance between patient self-assessment of current psoriasis disease burden, in line with
NICE guidelines digitised on mySkin, compared to standard of care.
Study 2 for Objective 2 (13 – 30 months): This validation study will use large-scale datasets to develop
an optimised model to predict psoriasis disease burden (combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic data),
using advanced statistical modelling and machine

liable digital tool that allows people with psoriasis to assess and predict if

Methods: First, I will recruit people with psoriasis across the UK from a variety of settings and
backgrounds (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and location) and invite them to self-assess how severe their
psoriasis is, on the mySkin app, using health information. I will invite 50 people from this group for an in-
person review by a skin specialist to test if they agree with the self-assessment.
Then, using large datasets of diverse populations, I will use different types of mathematical modelling to
predict who is likely to develop severe psoriasis. I will test and refine these predictions to improve my
model. This will be integrated onto a digital tool in the future.
Finally, I will host group discussions with people with psoriasis (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and
background) to understand how to communicate if a person has or will develop severe psoriasis back to
the individual. I will have group discussions with healthcare professionals to understand their views on

criteria.name: Quality of the Plain English Summary
criteria.definition: Clear, simple description of the research that a non-specialist audience can understand; explains problem, importance, plan, beneficiaries, and real-world use; avoids jargon; aligns with the detailed proposal.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)
Low density of unexplained jargon or technical terms
Presence of problem statement, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact
Alignment with detailed proposal content
Sentence structure and coherence for non-specialist understanding
Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary is highly readable (Flesch-Kincaid grade 7.8) with short sentences and clear structure. It comprehensively covers the problem (inequitable access to biologics), aim (digital stratification tool), methods (self-assessment, modelling, focus groups), beneficiaries (patients, clinicians), and impact (timely intervention). Jargon is minimal and well-explained (e.g., 'biologics' described as treatments that clear skin). The content aligns perfectly with the Scientific Abstract and Detailed Research Plan.
criteria.drawbacks: While the NLP analysis flags a 0.0 lexical overlap with the detailed plan, manual verification confirms strong semantic alignment; the low lexical score is likely due to the summary using simpler synonyms rather than technical terms. A few terms like 'mathematical modelling' are used without deep explanation, though context makes them understandable.
criteria.rationale: While the NLP analysis flags a 0.0 lexical overlap with the detailed plan, manual verification confirms strong semantic alignment; the low lexical score is likely due to the summary using simpler synonyms rather than technical terms. A few terms like 'mathematical modelling' are used without deep explanation, though context makes them understandable.

evidence.text:
Background: Psoriasis is a common, long-term skin condition that affects 1 in 50 people in the UK. It
causes irritating, red, scaly patches on the skin. About 1 in 3 people with psoriasis develop severe
psoriasis, which has a serious impact on health, wellbeing and daily life. Although there is no cure, new
treatments (“biologics”) for severe psoriasis can completely clear the skin. Unfortunately, despite
national guidelines, access to these treatments in the NHS is not equal and often delayed.
Research shows that starting biologics at the earliest opportunity can lead to better outcomes, fewer
psoriasis-related health problems and improved quality of life. Currently, we cannot predict who will
develop severe psoriasis in the future. However, we have recently shown that factors in the environment
and in genes can inform who will develop severe psoriasis in the future. At present, there are no reliable
tools in the NHS to assess and predict who has or will develop severe psoriasis, to help them get the
right treatment at the right time. I will build on our team’s existing smartphone app, mySkin (designed
with people with psoriasis), to address this gap.
Aim: To develop and test a re

evidence.text:
results back to
individuals.
Working with People and Communities: This project addresses key research priorities highlighted by
people with psoriasis. People with psoriasis have directly shaped this project. There was encouraging
support for a digital tool to support a personalised treatment approach in psoriasis using health and
genetic information. People with psoriasis and the public will advise on this project through 6-monthly
meetings. They will help plan and analyse the group discussions. They will also ensure this research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact/Dissemination: This project will support people with psoriasis to get equal access to the right
care at the right time, and improve long-term outcomes. It will allow healthcare professionals to provide
better care, tailored to individual needs to improve patient experience. I will share my findings in
scientific journals and at national/ international conferences. I will work with people with psoriasis to
create easily understood materials for circulation via multiple media platforms.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

criteria.pros: The application clearly identifies a specific knowledge gap: the lack of reliable tools to predict severe psoriasis and the inequity in current access to biologics. It critically analyzes the current 'one size fits all' approach as inefficient and delayed. The problem statement is well-grounded in the context of NICE guidelines and the need for proactive management.
criteria.drawbacks: The provided text (Abstract and Plain English Summary) does not contain a formal literature review with citations or a critical analysis of specific prior studies. The evidence review is implicit in the problem statement rather than explicit in a dedicated section, limiting the score for reference density and critical analysis depth.
criteria.rationale: The provided text (Abstract and Plain English Summary) does not contain a formal literature review with citations or a critical analysis of specific prior studies. The evidence review is implicit in the problem statement rather than explicit in a dedicated section, limiting the score for reference density and critical analysis depth.

2. Develop and validate a model to predict psoriasis disease burden, integrating clinical, lifestyle and
genetic data for future integration into a digital stratification tool.
3. Understand and optimise how current and predicted psoriasis disease burden is communicated to
patients using a digital stratification tool, through multi-stakeholder input.
This proposal has been shaped by people with psoriasis, their carers and healthcare professionals who
will be involved throughout to ensure inclusive research, relevant to diverse communities.
Methods and Timelines:
Study 1 for Objective 1 (0 – 12 months): This cross-sectional validation study will evaluate patient-
clinician concordance between patient self-assessment of current psoriasis disease burden, in line with
NICE guidelines digitised on mySkin, compared to standard of care.
Study 2 for Objective 2 (13 – 30 months): This validation study will use large-scale datasets to develop
an optimised model to predict psoriasis disease burden (combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic data),
using advanced statistical modelling and machine

learning approaches. External validation will be
performed in diverse datasets.
Study 3 for Objective 3 (11 – 36 months): This qualitative study will use focus groups with people with
psoriasis to explore attitudes towards communication of psoriasis disease burden. Focus groups with
healthcare professionals will explore attitudes towards patient self-assessment and tool deployment.
Insights will inform a communication interface prototype.
Impact:
A patient-held digital stratification tool for self-assessment of current and predicted psoriasis disease
burden could enable timely intervention, greater patient autonomy and improved outcomes, through
equitable access to care in line with NICE guidelines. This project aligns with NIHR James Lind Alliance
research priorities and the NHS Long Term Plan to support digitally-driven, proactive care.
Dissemination:
Findings will be published in open-access journals and presented at national/international conferences,
with co-developed Plain English Summaries for international multi-media dissemination via our
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
established networks.

evidence.text:
Background: Psoriasis is a common, life-long immune-mediated skin disease, affecting 2% of the adult
population. Most of the health, societal and economic burden is driven by the 30% of the population who
develop severe disease. Highly effective NICE-approved targeted biologic therapies mean that disease
remission is now achievable but our 'one size fits all', healthcare centric pathway of care means that
access is inequitable, inefficient and delayed. Evidence also suggests that earlier effective intervention
may further reduce impact on quality of life and psoriasis-associated multiple long-term conditions
(MLTC). Patient-centred strategies to better identify those with severe disease and predict those at risk
are now needed to improve access and enable proactive management, maximising the benefits of these
high-cost interventions, and thus improve patient outcomes. This proposal builds on our group’s existing
NIHR-funded smartphone app, mySkin, to address this gap.
Aims and Objectives:
My overarching aim is to develop and validate a patient-held digital stratification tool for psoriasis
disease burden.
To do this, I will:
1. Validate patient-driven self-assessment of current pso

psoriasis to explore attitudes towards communication of psoriasis disease burden. Focus groups with
healthcare professionals will explore attitudes towards patient self-assessment and tool deployment.
Insights will inform a communication interface prototype.
Impact:
A patient-held digital stratification tool for self-assessment of current and predicted psoriasis disease
burden could enable timely intervention, greater patient autonomy and improved outcomes, through
equitable access to care in line with NICE guidelines. This project aligns with NIHR James Lind Alliance
research priorities and the NHS Long Term Plan to support digitally-driven, proactive care.
Dissemination:
Findings will be published in open-access journals and presented at national/international conferences,
with co-developed Plain English Summaries for international multi-media dissemination via our
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
established networks.

individuals.
Working with People and Communities: This project addresses key research priorities highlighted by
people with psoriasis. People with psoriasis have directly shaped this project. There was encouraging
support for a digital tool to support a personalised treatment approach in psoriasis using health and
genetic information. People with psoriasis and the public will advise on this project through 6-monthly
meetings. They will help plan and analyse the group discussions. They will also ensure this research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact/Dissemination: This project will support people with psoriasis to get equal access to the right
care at the right time, and improve long-term outcomes. It will allow healthcare professionals to provide
better care, tailored to individual needs to improve patient experience. I will share my findings in
scientific journals and at national/ international conferences. I will work with people with psoriasis to
create easily understood materials for circulation via multiple media platforms.

evidence.text:
This proposal directly addresses five of the top 20 NIHR James Lind Alliance psoriasis research
priorities related to timely intervention and disease progression, to address significant unmet need.
I have co-developed all stages of my project alongside people with psoriasis, their carers and my patient
collaborator, Helen McAteer (Chief Executive, Psoriasis Association, the UK’s leading psoriasis patient
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
organisation) with diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, geographical location and psoriasis severity through:
• A 30 minute national Psoriasis Association webinar (n=69 attendees; 193 YouTube views)
• A national survey of people with psoriasis (n=73)
• A 1.5 hour in-person engagement group with people with psoriasis and their carers (n=11)
From these activities, four individuals with psoriasis (diverse in age, sex and ethnicity) have expressed
an interest in joining my Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG; total n=6). Additional recruitment will be
via the Psoriasis Association and our established PPI network to ensure diversity in views, background
and experience. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings at key decision points (in-
person

asis Association and my PLAG to inform diverse and
inclusive recruitment strategies to do this.
- 96% supported the use of genetic data for personalised psoriasis management advice, shaping the
inclusion of genetic information in my model in Objective 2. My PLAG will help to interpret model results.
External validation in diverse datasets will ensure generalisability and mitigate health inequity.
- 90% of people with psoriasis wanted to know their current and future psoriasis disease burden,
shaping the multistakeholder focus groups to understand and optimise this communication through a
digital stratification tool in Objective 3. PLAG members will receive qualitative research training, recruit
diverse focus groups participants (age, ethnicity, location) and interpret findings.
PPI contributors will receive training to co-develop Plain English summaries for multi-media
dissemination, co-author peer-reviewed publications and co-present at national events. PPI contributors
will be reimbursed in line with NIHR guidance. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI
activities.

criteria.name: Justification of Resources & Award Costs
criteria.definition: Appropriateness and clarity of requested resources for the planned work.

signals.signal_text: Explicit links between tasks and resources requested
Budget proportionality to project scope
Clear rationale for funding level

criteria.pros: The budget provides explicit links between tasks and resources: specific costs for the statistician, laptop for large datasets, training courses (Epidemiology, R, Qualitative), and PPIE activities (meetings, focus groups). The rationale for each cost is clear (e.g., high-performance laptop for genetic data analysis). The budget is proportional to the 3-year scope.
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the budget justification is comprehensive.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the budget justification is comprehensive.

evidence.text: All costs below are at 100% FEC (Cost to Funder). On the budget template, they take into account the 80% Price to Funder contribution. • Staff Costs • Salary (£271,522.63) Full-time salary for lead applicant for duration of the 3-year Doctoral Fellowship, based on nationally agreed NHS pay scales. • Part time statistician costs 0.1 WTE (£20,999.49) Funding is sought for a 0.1 WTE statistician to provide statistical oversight and guidance for the predictive modelling component and statistical analysis (which I will be carrying out myself) • Travel and Subsistence • Conferences (£3,000) Conference attendance costs for the British Association of Dermatologists Conference (BAD), the British Society for Investigative Dermatology (BSID) Conference, the European Society for Dermatological Research (ESDR) Conference, and the American Academy of Dermatology (AAD) Conference. • Placements (£7,500) NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Travel/accommodation costs for placements in my Career Development Plan. • Equipment • Laptop (£2,500) As my project involves working with very large datasets (including genetic data) and has a heavy analytical and model-fitting element, I will require a high-performan

ce laptop with a powerful CPU, plentiful RAM and at least 1TB of SSD storage. • Monitor (£500) and laptop accessories (£100) Funds for a secondary monitor considering the heavy programming component of my project and appropriate laptop accessories, including a keyboard, mouse and case. Training and Development • PhD Student Tuition Fees (£25,310) • Funds have been sought for the fees (and travel/accommodation where required) for the training courses outlined in my Career Development Plan: Year 1: Introductory Course in Epidemiology and Medical Statistics £3,700 Introduction to Qualitative Research Methods £2,000 Public Engagement Course £450 Introduction to Statistics £1,800 Statistical Computing with R £3,390 Clinical Prediction Rules £4,000 (£3,390 + travel/accommodation) Statistical Methods for Risk Prediction and Prognostic Models £499 Year 2: Genetic Epidemiology Course £1,100 The King’s Fund Emerging Clinical Leaders Course £2,700 Advanced Qualitative Research Methods £4,000 Year 3: Prognosis Research in Health Care £645 Digital Health Course £1,200 ESDR Future Leaders Academy - £800 inc travel/accommodation Genomic Medicine Counselling Skills £2000 (£1462 + travel/accommodat

ion) Knowledge into Action Course £4,000 (£3,390 + travel/accommodation) Introduction to Implementation Science £1,400 • Research Inclusion (£29,600) Data access and management for large-scale diverse ancestry datasets for Year 2/3 - Our Future Health (£10,800/year) and East London Genes & Health (£4,000/year) • PPIE • Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG) Meetings (£4,800) Funds for 6 participants to meet for 2 hours (in-person) at 6-monthly intervals with 2 additional meetings at key decision points (£50pp/meeting + £50 pp/meeting for travel reimbursement and refreshments for 8 times during the fellowship, in line with NIHR guidance) • PLAG co-facilitation of qualitative focus groups (£600) A trained PLAG member will cofacilitate each focus group for Objective 3 (6 sessions upto 2 hours in- person/online; £50pp/session + £50pp for travel reimbursement and refreshments in line with NIHR guidance) • Qualitative data analysis training for PPIE collaborators (£160) Half day online training session two PPIE collaborators (£75 + £5 connection charge, in line with NIHR guidance) • Qualitative Focus Groups (£3,600) Payment for qualitative focus groups for Objective 3 (36 individuals for

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

Adaptive methods or accessibility considerations
Equity and participation indicators

criteria.pros: Explicit strategies for diverse recruitment are detailed (age, ethnicity, sex, location) in both the Plain English Summary and PPI section. The proposal includes external validation in diverse datasets (Our Future Health, East London Genes & Health) to ensure generalisability. Accessibility is addressed via phone-in options for PPI and diverse focus group recruitment.
criteria.drawbacks: While strategies are strong, specific adaptive methods for participants with disabilities beyond digital exclusion (e.g., specific accommodations for the mySkin app interface for visually impaired users) are not explicitly detailed in the provided text.
criteria.rationale: While strategies are strong, specific adaptive methods for participants with disabilities beyond digital exclusion (e.g., specific accommodations for the mySkin app interface for visually impaired users) are not explicitly detailed in the provided text.

riasis disease burden, in line with NICE national
psoriasis guidelines, using the mySkin platform.
2. Develop and validate a model to predict psoriasis disease burden, integrating clinical, lifestyle and
genetic data for future integration into a digital stratification tool.
3. Understand and optimise how current and predicted psoriasis disease burden is communicated to
patients using a digital stratification tool, through multi-stakeholder input.
This proposal has been shaped by people with psoriasis, their carers and healthcare professionals who
will be involved throughout to ensure inclusive research, relevant to diverse communities.
Methods and Timelines:
Study 1 for Objective 1 (0 – 12 months): This cross-sectional validation study will evaluate patient-
clinician concordance between patient self-assessment of current psoriasis disease burden, in line with
NICE guidelines digitised on mySkin, compared to standard of care.
Study 2 for Objective 2 (13 – 30 months): This validation study will use large-scale datasets to develop
an optimised model to predict psoriasis disease burden (combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic data),
using advanced statistical modelling and machine

liable digital tool that allows people with psoriasis to assess and predict if
they have or will go on to develop severe psoriasis.
Methods: First, I will recruit people with psoriasis across the UK from a variety of settings and
backgrounds (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and location) and invite them to self-assess how severe their
psoriasis is, on the mySkin app, using health information. I will invite 50 people from this group for an in-
person review by a skin specialist to test if they agree with the self-assessment.
Then, using large datasets of diverse populations, I will use different types of mathematical modelling to
predict who is likely to develop severe psoriasis. I will test and refine these predictions to improve my
model. This will be integrated onto a digital tool in the future.
Finally, I will host group discussions with people with psoriasis (diverse in age, ethnicity, sex and
background) to understand how to communicate if a person has or will develop severe psoriasis back to
the individual. I will have group discussions with healthcare professionals to understand their views on
the tool. I will use this feedback to develop a preliminary platform to communicate

evidence.text:
This proposal directly addresses five of the top 20 NIHR James Lind Alliance psoriasis research
priorities related to timely intervention and disease progression, to address significant unmet need.
I have co-developed all stages of my project alongside people with psoriasis, their carers and my patient
collaborator, Helen McAteer (Chief Executive, Psoriasis Association, the UK’s leading psoriasis patient
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
organisation) with diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, geographical location and psoriasis severity through:
• A 30 minute national Psoriasis Association webinar (n=69 attendees; 193 YouTube views)
• A national survey of people with psoriasis (n=73)
• A 1.5 hour in-person engagement group with people with psoriasis and their carers (n=11)
From these activities, four individuals with psoriasis (diverse in age, sex and ethnicity) have expressed
an interest in joining my Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG; total n=6). Additional recruitment will be
via the Psoriasis Association and our established PPI network to ensure diversity in views, background
and experience. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings at key decision points (in-

sub_criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
sub_criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed and complete training plan in the budget section, listing specific courses (e.g., 'Introductory Course in Epidemiology', 'Statistical Computing with R', 'Genomic Medicine Counselling Skills') with exact costs. The training is clearly sequenced by year (Year 1, Year 2, Year 3), demonstrating a structured timeline. The courses directly align with the project's methodological needs, such as predictive modelling, qualitative research, and genetic epidemiology.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the list of courses is comprehensive, the explicit link between specific course content and the exact skill acquisition for each project objective is implied through the course titles rather than explicitly described in a narrative training plan. The evidence is primarily found in the budget justification rather than a dedicated training narrative section.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the list of courses is comprehensive, the explicit link between specific course content and the exact skill acquisition for each project objective is implied through the course titles rather than explicitly described in a narrative training plan. The evidence is primarily found in the budget justification rather than a dedicated training narrative section.
