sub_criteria.name: Suitability to Personal Development and Project Needs
sub_criteria.definition: How well the proposed training addresses the applicant’s current skills gaps and supports successful project delivery.

signals.signal_text: Identification of personal skill gaps
Clear mapping between training activities and project tasks
Evidence that proposed training is targeted rather than generic
Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

sub_criteria.pros: The training is highly targeted to project tasks: statistical computing and risk prediction courses support the modelling component, while qualitative methods courses support the focus group work. The budget explicitly funds these specific courses, and the Application Context confirms supervisors will provide formal training in bioinformatics and statistics. The plan includes a mix of formal courses, workshops, and mentorship.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The application does not explicitly articulate the applicant's current skill gaps in a narrative format; instead, the gaps are inferred from the selection of training courses. While the mapping to project tasks is strong, the explicit statement of 'what I lack' is less direct than the statement of 'what I will learn'.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.name: Support for Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: The extent to which the training plan, and any professional practice components, contribute to the applicant’s long-term career goals.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of career goals
Connections between training activities and future career trajectory
Indications of skill transferability beyond the current project
Reference to professional practice or experiential learning where applicable

sub_criteria.pros: The training plan includes specific leadership and career development courses such as 'The King’s Fund Emerging Clinical Leaders Course', 'ESDR Future Leaders Academy', and 'Introduction to Implementation Science', which directly support long-term career aspirations in research leadership and translation. The Application Context mentions the goal to transition to postdoctoral research, and the training supports this trajectory.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: There is no explicit, standalone statement of long-term career goals within the training section itself; the career aspirations are primarily found in the Application Context (supervisor descriptions) and inferred from the course titles. The connection between the training and the specific future career trajectory is strong but relies on inference from the course names rather than a direct narrative link in the training plan.
sub_criteria.rationale: There is no explicit, standalone statement of long-term career goals within the training section itself; the career aspirations are primarily found in the Application Context (supervisor descriptions) and inferred from the course titles. The connection between the training and the specific future career trajectory is strong but relies on inference from the course names rather than a direct narrative link in the training plan.

NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed and complete training plan in the budget section, listing specific courses (e.g., 'Introductory Course in Epidemiology', 'Statistical Computing with R', 'Genomic Medicine Counselling Skills') with exact costs. The training is clearly sequenced by year (Year 1, Year 2, Year 3), demonstrating a structured timeline. The courses directly align with the project's methodological needs, such as predictive modelling, qualitative research, and genetic epidemiology.
criteria.drawbacks: While the list of courses is comprehensive, the explicit link between specific course content and the exact skill acquisition for each project objective is implied through the course titles rather than explicitly described in a narrative training plan. The evidence is primarily found in the budget justification rather than a dedicated training narrative section.
criteria.rationale: While the list of courses is comprehensive, the explicit link between specific course content and the exact skill acquisition for each project objective is implied through the course titles rather than explicitly described in a narrative training plan. The evidence is primarily found in the budget justification rather than a dedicated training narrative section.

criteria.name: Suitability to Personal Development and Project Needs
criteria.definition: How well the proposed training addresses the applicant’s current skills gaps and supports successful project delivery.

signals.signal_text: Identification of personal skill gaps
Clear mapping between training activities and project tasks
Evidence that proposed training is targeted rather than generic
Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

criteria.pros: The training is highly targeted to project tasks: statistical computing and risk prediction courses support the modelling component, while qualitative methods courses support the focus group work. The budget explicitly funds these specific courses, and the Application Context confirms supervisors will provide formal training in bioinformatics and statistics. The plan includes a mix of formal courses, workshops, and mentorship.
criteria.drawbacks: The application does not explicitly articulate the applicant's current skill gaps in a narrative format; instead, the gaps are inferred from the selection of training courses. While the mapping to project tasks is strong, the explicit statement of 'what I lack' is less direct than the statement of 'what I will learn'.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. criteria.name: Support for Career Aspirations
criteria.definition: The extent to which the training plan, and any professional practice components, contribute to the applicant’s long-term career goals.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of career goals
Connections between training activities and future career trajectory
Indications of skill transferability beyond the current project
Reference to professional practice or experiential learning where applicable

criteria.pros: The training plan includes specific leadership and career development courses such as 'The King’s Fund Emerging Clinical Leaders Course', 'ESDR Future Leaders Academy', and 'Introduction to Implementation Science', which directly support long-term career aspirations in research leadership and translation. The Application Context mentions the goal to transition to postdoctoral research, and the training supports this trajectory.
criteria.drawbacks: There is no explicit, standalone statement of long-term career goals within the training section itself; the career aspirations are primarily found in the Application Context (supervisor descriptions) and inferred from the course titles. The connection between the training and the specific future career trajectory is strong but relies on inference from the course names rather than a direct narrative link in the training plan.
criteria.rationale: There is no explicit, standalone statement of long-term career goals within the training section itself; the career aspirations are primarily found in the Application Context (supervisor descriptions) and inferred from the course titles. The connection between the training and the specific future career trajectory is strong but relies on inference from the course names rather than a direct narrative link in the training plan.

NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.name: Track Record of the Contracting Organisation (and Partners)
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides extensive evidence of the contracting organisation's (King's College London) and partners' track record. Supervisors are described as world-leading experts with specific achievements: Professor Langan leads Health Data Research UK London; Professor Smith chairs NICE and BAD guideline committees and leads national registries (BADBIR, BSTOP); Professor Simpson led an Innovate UK project; Dr Mahil is Chief Investigator of the mySkin study. These details strongly support publication history, funding success, collaborations, and notable outputs.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the track record of the individuals is exceptional, the text focuses heavily on the supervisors' personal achievements rather than explicitly listing institutional-level metrics (e.g., total departmental funding or specific institutional awards) separate from the individuals, though the individuals' roles effectively demonstrate the organisation's strength.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the track record of the individuals is exceptional, the text focuses heavily on the supervisors' personal achievements rather than explicitly listing institutional-level metrics (e.g., total departmental funding or specific institutional awards) separate from the individuals, though the individuals' roles effectively demonstrate the organisation's strength.

0009-0002-9163-1264

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: The application details a robust supervisory team of four (one mentor, three supervisors) with explicit roles and responsibilities. Specific meeting frequencies are provided for each (weekly, fortnightly, monthly, every two months). The expertise of each supervisor is directly aligned with project needs (e.g., Professor Simpson for genetics/statistics, Dr Mahil for digital health, Professor Smith for clinical relevance). The text explicitly mentions training plans, including bioinformatics, statistics, and career development support.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details regarding the supervision framework; the plan is comprehensive and specific.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details regarding the supervision framework; the plan is comprehensive and specific.

sub_criteria.name: Appropriateness of the Contracting Organisation to Support the Award and Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

sub_criteria.pros: The application clearly links organisational support to project success, citing access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn, Estonian Biobank) via Professor Smith's EU BIOMAP consortium role and the mySkin platform via Dr Mahil. The Integrated Academic Training Programme at St John's Institute of Dermatology is highlighted as a resource for career development. The alignment between the organisation's focus (dermatology, genetics, health data) and the applicant's trajectory is explicit.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While access to datasets and networks is well-described, specific details about physical facilities (e.g., lab space, computing infrastructure) are less explicit compared to the human and data resources, though implied through the supervisors' roles.
sub_criteria.rationale: While access to datasets and networks is well-described, specific details about physical facilities (e.g., lab space, computing infrastructure) are less explicit compared to the human and data resources, though implied through the supervisors' roles.

Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Inclusive and Supportive Research Culture
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that the organisation fosters inclusivity, research integrity, and a supportive environment for all researchers.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of policies or practices promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion
Evidence of inclusive recruitment, mentoring, or support initiatives
References to adherence to research integrity principles
Statements on organisational culture, training, or initiatives supporting underrepresented groups

sub_criteria.pros: The application mentions the 'Integrated Academic Training Programme' and 'DermAcademy' as platforms for education and development, suggesting a supportive environment. The diversity of the supervisory team (gender balance, multidisciplinary expertise) is evident. The text notes the applicant's transition to postdoctoral research within a supportive mentorship structure.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: There is limited explicit mention of specific institutional policies regarding diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) or research integrity principles. The evidence for a supportive culture is inferred from the existence of training programmes and the team composition rather than direct statements of organisational culture or specific initiatives for underrepresented groups.
sub_criteria.rationale: There is limited explicit mention of specific institutional policies regarding diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) or research integrity principles. The evidence for a supportive culture is inferred from the existence of training programmes and the team composition rather than direct statements of organisational culture or specific initiatives for underrepresented groups.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

criteria.name: Track Record of the Contracting Organisation (and Partners)
criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

criteria.pros: The application provides extensive evidence of the contracting organisation's (King's College London) and partners' track record. Supervisors are described as world-leading experts with specific achievements: Professor Langan leads Health Data Research UK London; Professor Smith chairs NICE and BAD guideline committees and leads national registries (BADBIR, BSTOP); Professor Simpson led an Innovate UK project; Dr Mahil is Chief Investigator of the mySkin study. These details strongly support publication history, funding success, collaborations, and notable outputs.
criteria.drawbacks: While the track record of the individuals is exceptional, the text focuses heavily on the supervisors' personal achievements rather than explicitly listing institutional-level metrics (e.g., total departmental funding or specific institutional awards) separate from the individuals, though the individuals' roles effectively demonstrate the organisation's strength.
criteria.rationale: While the track record of the individuals is exceptional, the text focuses heavily on the supervisors' personal achievements rather than explicitly listing institutional-level metrics (e.g., total departmental funding or specific institutional awards) separate from the individuals, though the individuals' roles effectively demonstrate the organisation's strength.

criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

criteria.pros: The application details a robust supervisory team of four (one mentor, three supervisors) with explicit roles and responsibilities. Specific meeting frequencies are provided for each (weekly, fortnightly, monthly, every two months). The expertise of each supervisor is directly aligned with project needs (e.g., Professor Simpson for genetics/statistics, Dr Mahil for digital health, Professor Smith for clinical relevance). The text explicitly mentions training plans, including bioinformatics, statistics, and career development support.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details regarding the supervision framework; the plan is comprehensive and specific.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details regarding the supervision framework; the plan is comprehensive and specific.

NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

criteria.pros: The application clearly links organisational support to project success, citing access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn, Estonian Biobank) via Professor Smith's EU BIOMAP consortium role and the mySkin platform via Dr Mahil. The Integrated Academic Training Programme at St John's Institute of Dermatology is highlighted as a resource for career development. The alignment between the organisation's focus (dermatology, genetics, health data) and the applicant's trajectory is explicit.
criteria.drawbacks: While access to datasets and networks is well-described, specific details about physical facilities (e.g., lab space, computing infrastructure) are less explicit compared to the human and data resources, though implied through the supervisors' roles.
criteria.rationale: While access to datasets and networks is well-described, specific details about physical facilities (e.g., lab space, computing infrastructure) are less explicit compared to the human and data resources, though implied through the supervisors' roles.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. criteria.name: Commitment to Inclusive and Supportive Research Culture
criteria.definition: Evidence that the organisation fosters inclusivity, research integrity, and a supportive environment for all researchers.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of policies or practices promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion
Evidence of inclusive recruitment, mentoring, or support initiatives
References to adherence to research integrity principles
Statements on organisational culture, training, or initiatives supporting underrepresented groups

criteria.pros: The application mentions the 'Integrated Academic Training Programme' and 'DermAcademy' as platforms for education and development, suggesting a supportive environment. The diversity of the supervisory team (gender balance, multidisciplinary expertise) is evident. The text notes the applicant's transition to postdoctoral research within a supportive mentorship structure.
criteria.drawbacks: There is limited explicit mention of specific institutional policies regarding diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) or research integrity principles. The evidence for a supportive culture is inferred from the existence of training programmes and the team composition rather than direct statements of organisational culture or specific initiatives for underrepresented groups.
criteria.rationale: There is limited explicit mention of specific institutional policies regarding diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) or research integrity principles. The evidence for a supportive culture is inferred from the existence of training programmes and the team composition rather than direct statements of organisational culture or specific initiatives for underrepresented groups.
